info.name: Shape Shifter
info.attribution:
Design, layout, cards, and library image by LawDawg and released to the Public Domain under CC0.

All icons and graphics in the room have their attribution listed under the About overlay.
info.description: Each of eight shapes has a random set of rules that must be followed when placing on your board. Can you get all shapes on the board before time runs out?
info.similarAwards:
2013/Spring Parents' Choice Gold Award Winner
2012 MinD-Spieletipp Winner
2012 Guldbrikken Best Adult Game Nominee
2012 Golden Geek Best Abstract Board Game Nominee
2011 Spiel des Jahres Recommended
2011 Meeples Choice Award Nominee
2011 Kinderspielexperten "8-to-13-year-olds" Nominee
2011 Deutscher Lernspielpreis "9 years and up" Nominee
2011 Årets Spill Best Family Game Nominee
info.helpText:
Players take seats and click the restart button to begin. Choose number number of minutes to put on the timer (default is 1).

Use the numbered blue buttons to deal cards for the level of difficulty (1-5) you desire on the top row. Use the bottom row if using one the rules variants to handicap players or create two simultaneous conditions for some shapes.

Information about the symbols used to represent the rules can be found in the hoverable player aids in the bottom right.

Click the play arrow to start the timer.

Drag and drop pieces onto your board. Only you can see your board.

When the timer runs out, you can click the eye icons next to a seat to show a copy of that board (that no one can interact with) to all players.

Use the +/- buttons or click on the penalty button to change score.

Press the numbered blue buttons to deal the next group of cards and press the play arrow to start the timer. Continue for as many rounds as you want.

fields.label: Type the number of points gained or lost. Put a - before the number to lose points.

clickRoutine: // If you always want this seat to be a specific color, add a `staticColor` property wtih the hex color as the value

// If you want this seat to do other things, scroll all the way to the bottom and lock for comments about adding functions depending on whether the seat is being used or not. If you need functions that activate whenever there is a status change (being used or not), add those at the end of this clickRoutine.
// Check if another player is picking this seat

thenRoutine: // If another player is currently picking this seat, notify the play and end

fields.text: Pick another seat. Another player is getting this one ready.

elseRoutine: // If another player does not have the INPUT overlay on this seat active then flag it as active and CALL the routines needed to use the seat
// Update player color and name to match player's selections.

colorContrastRoutine: // Use color functions to automatically adjust contrast for best visibility
doubleClickRoutine: // A quick way to enter a seat with current playerName and random available color.
updateColorArrayRoutine: // Select all active seats except the current one and get color into an array.
// Remove colors used in other seats from the list of available colors for this player's overlay choices

thenRoutine: // If seat is empty, then prompt for name (and color if not static).

elseRoutine.header: Enter your name and choice of player color.

thenRoutine: // If seat is occupied by the player pressing the button, allow player to change name, color if not static, or get up from seat.

elseRoutine.header: Enter/change your name and choice of player color (or leave seat).

elseRoutine: // If seat is occupied by other than the player clicking, then confirm removal.

elseRoutine.header: Are you sure you want to remove ${PROPERTY player} from this seat?

elseRoutine: // Evaluate scenarios for what properties need to change

thenRoutine: // Double check to make sure that another player has not chosen the same color if multiple seat INPUT overlays were open at the same time
// Make updates to player color and name array for the return to the clickRoutine and SET the properties on this seat

elseRoutine: // If there is anything else this seat needs to do after it has been claimed, do that here.

clickRoutine: var index = ${thisID} charAt 0
var index = ${thisID} charAt 0

#enlarged.left: calc(var(--roomLeft) + (max(${PROPERTY enlargeX}px, 0px) + min(1px, max(0px, (0px - ${PROPERTY enlargeX}px))) * (800 - ${PROPERTY enlargeWidth} / 2) + min(1px, max(0px, (-1px - ${PROPERTY enlargeX}px))) * ((1600 - ${PROPERTY enlargeWidth}) - (800 - ${PROPERTY enlargeWidth} / 2))) * var(--scale))

level1-3.detail:
Overview

Each level of card (1-5) is indicated on the back. On the front, in the upper left corner is a number and sometimes a letter. The details for levels 1-3 are described below. Levels 4-5 are on another hoverable player aid.

Level 1

This pawn can be placed anywhere on the board.

Level 2

This pawn can be placed anywhere on the board that corresponds to the dark brown circles on the card.

Level 3

There are three types of these cards. The lines in blue on the images show what is meant by adjacent, across, and sharing a corner.

3a Adjacent: This pawn must be next to the color shown on the card.

3b Across: This pawn must be across from the color shown on the card.

3c Sharing a corner: This pawn must share a corner with the color shown on the card.

level4-5.detail:
Overview

Each level of card (1-5) is indicated on the back. On the front, in the upper left corner is a number and sometimes a letter. The details for levels 4-5 are described below. Levels 1-3 are on another hoverable player aid.

Level 4

There are three types of these cards.

4a: This pawn must do the same thing as the color shown on the card.
4b: This pawn cannot be adjacent to or across from the color shown on the card. Look at the level 3 cards for an image of what is meant by adjacent and across.
4c: This pawn must be at least two spaces away from the color on the card.
Level 5

This pawn must do the opposite of the whatever the color on the card is doing.

Leval 1: There is no opposite so the pawn can be placed anywhere on the board.
Level 2: Opposite means the light-colored spaces on the card.
Level 3: Opposite means any space that does not meet the criteria for the card (adjacent, across, or sharing a corner).
Level 4: Opposite for 4a cards means doing the opposite of the color on the card. Opposite for 4b cards means adjacent to or across from the color on the card. Opposite for 4c cards means not two spaces away from the color on the card.
Level 5: Opposite means the same as the color on the card.

clickRoutine: // Used to reset the same ready for the PL
